titleStmt.title: Letter from Kuno Meyer to Roger Casement, 15 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Kuno Meyer

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: The letter is from Kuna Meyer (1858-1919) to Sir Roger Casement (1864-1916). Meyer was a German philologist and scholar who had founded the School of Irish Learning to promote the Irish language and was a close friend of Casement. The letter was written to Casement while he was in Germany. Casement spent eighteen months in Germany, arriving first as an envoy of Irish-American leaders, attempting to encourage Germany to support Irish separatist aspirations by providing arms. He succeeded in securing limited German support but his attempt to form a brigade of Irish soldiers in German prisoner of war camps to fight against Britain was largely unsuccessful. Believing the German support was insufficient, Casement travelled to Ireland to prevent the planned rebellion but was arrested after in Kerry on the eve of the Easter Rising. He was hanged in London in August 1916. Meyer, whose pro-German stance was unpopular in the US (from where he writes), refers to American public interest in World War I and is critical of both the British secret service and the English people, whose he describes as 'vicious cowards'. He finishes by informing Casement of his marriage to a women of Scots-Irish descent and his plans to continue lecturing after an accident.

msIdentifier.collection: Roger Casement Papers, Collection List A15; Ms. 10,073/10iii

correspAction.placeName: Arrowhead Hot Springs Hotel, California, US

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
R. 19.1.16
This is Irish of
"My sweet friend" Arrowhead Hot Springs Hotel
H. M., NICKERSON, MANAGER
ARROWHEAD SPRINGS, CALIFORNIA 15/1.16 Mo chara dhílis,</hi> Our correspondence has
become very irregular, but I believe that all our
letters have got through. Meanwhile I hear of you
both from Germany through my brother & Schiemann &
on this side from several of yr correspondents.
Your various articles in the Cont. Times & c. have
been widely reprinted here & have had much effect
on people who still use their own judgement. On
the whole however the interest in the war has
greatly decreased here. I also notice that the vehement
partisanship of a year ago has abated much. The
belief that England is losing her game is spreading
in ever wider circles. I have had some interesting
correspondence with Roosevelt, who writes that the
thought of the loss of so many of his former
German friends is a genuine grief to him. The British secret service is as
active & futile as ever. You cannot imagine what
silly things they do & make the American govt do.
Some time ago they came to the conclusion that
Germany was looking for a submarine base some-
where on the coast of Maine. So they spread a
network of secret service men all over that part
of the country. Among others, my friend Kommer,
whose delightful exposal of the "Morning Posts"
daily despatches from Budapest you will have
read, was for 3 days under surveillance when
he stayed at a hotel in Monhegan
Island. It makes a most amusing story which
he must write some day. I often hear from Ireland, but you will be
as well posted as I, or better. I still correspond
with many old English friends, who all give the
most woful accounts of conditions there. The
ignorance about the resources of Germany and the
conditions prevailing there is as great as ever, and
the silliest lies are still believed. But I know
their minds & temper too well to be astonished
at anything. Looking back on my English life I can
hardly recall mor than a dozen really intelligent
& well informed men, whose character was above
reproach. Moral cowardice in public & private
life was always the curse of England, & you know
how vicious cowards can be. Lying has become an
art with them & he who can best handle cant is
always sure of a following. It was so in Munich,
city life, & you know better than I that it was
so in politics. Add to this, as friend Yeats
that the conversation of an Englishman is
one continuous swagger, and you have all the
elements of their present doings. Monroe's
report on the evacuation of Gallipoli with "one
man wounded" was a delightful example the other
day. To me the whole bitter fight between Germany
& England is rather ethical than physical, and
that from the outset never made me doubt for
one moment what the issue would be. Your
characterisation of Grey interested me
greatly. I have known dozens of men like him. My own activities here came to a temporary
standstill through my accident. But now that I
have recovered I shall resume them. I dare say you
heard of my marriage. My wife is of Scots-Irish
origin & was a good pro-German even before I
made her acquaintance. The only thing I have
done lately is writing for the press. I hope to
continue my lectures among Anglo-American
circles whom I can easily meet on their own
ground through my superior knowledge of
England & the English language. As for the Irish,
they need no conversion, as you know. It was
amusing to see how the Aberdeens were given
the cold shoulder here wherever they went, and
Lady Gregory has not fared much better. I have had some delightful visits from
old English friends, Sir Walter Raleigh and
Prof. W.. H. Young, one of the foremost mathe-
maticians of England, among the number. Write again. My best address is always
c/o L. Guenzel, 332, So. Michigan Ave. Chicago. Mise do chara Kuno Meyer
